entities.name: Webflow
entities.how_described: 本集嘉宾 Jessica Fain 目前任职产品负责人的公司，集里提及了该公司的 Office Hours (办公时间) 制度，以及内部探讨技能普及化（人人皆可写代码、写 PRD）的背景。

evidence.quote: Today, my guest is Jessica Fain, who's been a product leader at Box and Slack and Brightwheel, and now at Webflow.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So we have something at Webflow called Office Hours.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Just last week, we've been working a lot on as skills get democratized across Webflow, everyone can do design, everyone can ship code, everyone can write a PRD.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: Slack
entities.how_described: 嘉宾曾担任产品经理与 CPO 幕僚长的公司。她在 Slack 近距离观察了高管如何做决策，并主导了核心产品团队和名为“客户爱冲刺”的内部修复改进运动。

evidence.quote: Today, my guest is Jessica Fain, who's been a product leader at Box and Slack and Brightwheel, and now at Webflow.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And at the time that I'm thinking about, I was a PM at Slack, and some of my ideas got people really excited, got me funding and backing, and just really a sense that we could accomplish something great.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And I was eight months pregnant, eight and a half months pregnant, April Underwood, who had just been named CPO of Slack.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Noah Weiss, who succeeded Tamar Yehoshua, CPO of Slack and also former podcast guest.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: We sat down to write product principles for Slack.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And Ethan Eisman, our head of design, was leading that initiative, but really this was a lot about codifying the early ideas that made Slack great, especially from a product principles and sort of craft perspective.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And we started talking about this and Noah had been at Slack early on, and he pulls out a small notebook that he kept in his bag and he said, "I actually have a section of this notebook that I've been keeping things I learned from Stuart over the years."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And we worked with a lot of the sort of early Slack folks and the people that had really developed that culture, but it was folks who had really tried to grow and learn.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I feel like just the alumni of Slack, holy moly, just like this is a...
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Elon Frank, who was my boss at Slack as well, is now CPO of Checker, he always had a customer anecdote in his back pocket.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: He always brought his expertise to bear, and many of the executives at Slack were not from a strong enterprise background, so he could bring that to the forefront.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: We implemented something at Slack with Stuart, because we realized we were coming to him with done designs and he was like, "What the fuck is this?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So Allie Rail, early Slack employee implemented something called, "Hey, Stuart, what do you think?"
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So after I was chief of staff, I took over Slack's core product team.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: That was one of the things that made Slack great in the first place was just it really felt like a product that took that usability to a 10X place.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: Box
entities.how_described: 嘉宾早期的雇主之一，她在此担任 PM，并在本期分享了她的第一任经理 Annie Pearl 如何教导她高管视角与向上沟通的责任意识。

evidence.quote: Today, my guest is Jessica Fain, who's been a product leader at Box and Slack and Brightwheel, and now at Webflow.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And Annie Pearl, who was my first PM manager back at Box.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And Jeff and I worked together at Box.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: 智能体 (agents)
entities.how_described: 本集讨论 AI 时代时指出，随着智能体像同事一样加入团队，你需要像给新员工做入职培训一样，把自己脑子里的目标、产品哲学和护栏明确写进设定里去影响这些不知疲倦的 AI。

evidence.quote: Fire off a hundred agents and we'll go build it and launch it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Maybe one more question along these lines, just agents now running as colleagues.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Now that agents are a part of the workforce and increasingly, is there anything there around influence that is valuable for people to start thinking about to learn how to do?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We talk about this a lot, especially when we think about identity, security, compliance, that agents are a teammate for accelerating work, and the risks that that also provides because of the places where agents fall down.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I think agents are absolutely something we have to think about with relation to influence, because we are basically all directors of work now, right?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: If we have an army of agents or this hundred new colleagues that joined our team, how would we onboard new junior team members who don't already understand our product philosophy, what's important to us, and how do we codify that for ourselves?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: You should just feed this podcast to a bunch of agents and see if they can do this better than me, see what their advice is.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: No, but I really think at this point we are in a place where it is a back and forth and where we should be using agents and AI as a really smart colleague that never gets irritated by our questions.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: We'll add that to the..." All the ideas you've shared, agents are so good at.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so I think one of the ways that we actually have to use AI and agents is to help our brains be most effective, is to clear out distraction, to point out the most important things, to... And I think this is where people really need to make some tough decisions.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: 护栏 (guardrails)
entities.how_described: 嘉宾在谈及使用智能体时提到，由于 AI 会产生幻觉或失误，人类需要设定好护栏，明确指出在哪些需要人类独特品味和判断力的地方，AI 不能脱离控制“独自完成”。

evidence.quote: But I also think the flip side of this is guardrails.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: 产品市场契合度 (product market fit)
entities.how_described: 本集指出，随着执行层面的难度大幅下降，人们将更容易通过测试迭代获得产品市场契合度的早期信号，但这也让决定什么东西值得大规模推进变得更为关键。

evidence.quote: My podcast guest and I love talking about craft and taste and agency and product market fit.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I actually think it's just so incredibly exciting because PMs and other functions, engineers, designers are so empowered to bring that first version to bear to get that momentum, to get that signal on product market fit, on user benefit, and then build momentum from that point in a much faster cycle.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: What do I believe about product market fit?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0
